annotations.text: The localizing value of this patient’s cognitive symptoms and signs depends on the aspects of cognition that are affected.

Disturbances of these functions may result from lesions involving the frontal lobes, but they can also be manifestations of multifocal cortical disease or of lesions involving the white-matter tracts, deep nuclei, or cerebellum.

This makes many diagnoses unlikely, including conditions that have a rapid time course (e.g., Creutzfeldt–Jakob disease, Hashimoto’s encephalopathy, and many neoplastic diseases) and conditions that cause recurrent or discrete events (e.g., stroke and multiple sclerosis).

Several categories of disorders remain, including psychiatric diseases, medical diseases with central nervous system (CNS) involvement, and neurologic diseases, such as degenerative disease, cerebral microvascular disease, and immunologic disease or cancer with neurologic effects.
Although diabetes-associated microvascular disease is a diagnostic consideration in this case, the findings on MRI are not consistent with this diagnosis.
The combination of hypertensive vasculopathy and cerebral amyloid angiopathy would not be expected to occur in such a symmetric distribution.
The most likely explanation of this patient’s abnormalities on CT and MRI is excessive mineralization, most commonly of calcium, copper, manganese, or iron.

The neurologic diseases that are most likely to be the cause of this patient’s gradual cognitive decline include cerebral microvascular disease, degenerative disease (e.g., early-onset Alzheimer’s disease, various forms of frontotemporal dementia, Parkinson’s disease, and Lewy body disease), normal-pressure hydrocephalus, slow-growing cancer, and some infectious diseases.

On the basis of this patient’s clinical presentation, history of gradual cognitive decline, imaging findings consistent with excessive mineralization, and low serum levels of iron and total iron-binding capacity with a high ferritin level, I think aceruloplasminemia is the most likely diagnosis in this case.

To establish this diagnosis, I would obtain the serum ceruloplasmin level, which I expect would be undetectable, and if so, I would perform genetic testing to identify mutations in the ceruloplasmin gene.
The classic clinical triad of aceruloplasminemia includes neurologic dysfunction, retinal degeneration, and diabetes mellitus due to iron deposition in the brain, retina, and pancreas, respectively.

The absence of functional ceruloplasmin leads to derangements in iron efflux and transport, thereby leading to a high ferritin level with paradoxically low levels of iron and total iron-binding capacity.
A diagnosis of hereditary aceruloplasminemia is consistent with all these laboratory values.

The patient had a compound heterozygous mutation in the ceruloplasmin gene, also known as the ferroxidase or iron (II):oxygen oxidoreductase gene, which led to the following amino acid changes: p.Arg215* and p.Gly195Arg.

Thus, we needed to initiate iron chelation therapy to reduce iron overload and control systemic iron deposition while remaining cognizant of her existing iron deficiency and iron-restricted erythropoiesis.

Although the efficacy of iron chelation therapy in patients with brain iron accumulation and neurologic manifestations is controversial, we initially treated this patient with 5 days of deferoxamine and then transitioned to deferiprone, which is the only iron chelator that is able to cross the blood–brain barrier.
